meta.title: Sachin Rangaswamy — Computational Materials Scientist
meta.description: Research Scientist at Leibniz IWT Bremen. Multiscale simulation, physics-informed machine learning and materials design — from electronic structure to engineering scale.

about.title: About
about.heading: Connecting physics to engineering, one scale at a time
about.lead: I am a computational materials scientist at the Leibniz Institute for Materials Engineering (Leibniz IWT) and PhD candidate at the University of Bremen, working on AI-guided computational materials design for sustainable manufacturing.
about.body: My research links the quantum world to the engineering world: density functional theory and electronic-structure methods describe bonding and energetics; machine-learning interatomic potentials and molecular dynamics resolve atomic motion; CALPHAD thermodynamics, kinetics and phase-field models capture microstructure evolution; and continuum methods translate all of it into component-level performance. Physics-informed machine learning is the thread that stitches these scales together into predictive, interpretable materials-design workflows.

scales.title: A journey through the length scales of matter
scales.intro: Every material property an engineer measures is born in the quantum world. Scroll to travel from the wavefunction to the finished component — the same journey information takes in a multiscale simulation.
scales.hoverHint: Hover a scale to reveal its methods

quantum.name: Quantum Scale
quantum.description: At the smallest scale, materials are electrons and nuclei governed by the Schrödinger equation. Density functional theory makes this tractable: the ground-state energy is a functional of the electron density, giving first-principles access to bonding, formation energies, band structures and charge densities — no experimental input required.

disorder.name: Disordered Alloys
disorder.description: Real engineering alloys are chemically disordered — atoms of different species share the same lattice randomly. The exact muffin-tin orbital method with the coherent potential approximation (EMTO-CPA) computes the electronic structure of such random alloys directly, predicting how disorder shapes stability, elasticity and magnetism in multicomponent systems.

atomistic.name: Atomistic Scale
atomistic.description: Millions of atoms move under Newton's equations, integrated step by step with the velocity-Verlet algorithm. Machine-learning interatomic potentials, trained on DFT data, deliver near-quantum accuracy at a millionth of the cost — resolving diffusion pathways, defect dynamics and interface behaviour inaccessible to either DFT or experiment.

micro.name: Microstructure Scale
micro.description: Properties are made in the microstructure. CALPHAD thermodynamics assembles Gibbs-energy databases from first-principles and experimental data to predict phase diagrams; phase-field models evolve that free energy in space and time, simulating solidification, precipitation and grain growth under real processing conditions.
micro.transfer: Microstructure state maps to local properties

continuum.name: Continuum Scale
continuum.description: Heat, mass and momentum transport during processing set the boundary conditions for everything below. Computational fluid dynamics solves the conservation equations of continuum mechanics — melt-pool convection, gas flow, thermal gradients — connecting process parameters to the local thermal histories that drive microstructure formation.
continuum.transfer: Thermal & mechanical fields load the component

engineering.name: Engineering Scale
engineering.description: Finally, the finite element method discretizes a real component into elements and solves for stress, strain and temperature under service loads. Here the entire chain pays off: microstructure-informed constitutive laws let a component-level simulation carry quantum-level physics — closing the loop from electrons to engineering.

scales.outro: Design signals flow down as requirements; physics flows up as properties. Bridging both directions is the essence of computational materials design.
